subject: Astronomy & Space
title: Direct Imaging of Exoplanets
description: A tiny lesson with a big payoff: Direct Imaging of Exoplanets.

hook.text: Taking a photo of an exoplanet is incredibly hard. Stars are billions of times brighter than their planets.

buildup.text: Coronagraphs block the star's light, revealing faint planets nearby. It's like spotting a firefly next to a searchlight.

discovery.text: The first direct image came in 2004—a planet orbiting the brown dwarf 2M1207. It was a historic achievement.

twist.text: Direct imaging works best for young, hot, massive planets far from their stars. Small rocky worlds remain invisible.

climax.text: JWST's coronagraph and future missions like HWO aim to directly photograph Earth-like planets for the first time.

punchline.text: A firefly beside a searchlight. We're learning to see it clearly.

quiz.question: Why is direct imaging of exoplanets so difficult?
options: Stars are billions of times brighter than their planets, overwhelming the signal
Exoplanets move too quickly to be captured by cameras
The distances involved are too great for light to reach us
